questions.question: Which Notion plan provides access to the custom data retention settings feature?

options.text: Free Plan
options.explanation: The feature is not available on the Free Plan.

options.text: Personal Pro Plan
options.explanation: Only Enterprise workspaces have this capability.

options.text: Enterprise Plan
options.explanation: Custom data retention settings are exclusive to the Enterprise Plan.

options.text: Team Plan
options.explanation: Team Plan does not include this feature.

questions.question: What is the default number of days a page stays in Trash before permanent deletion?

options.text: 7 days
options.explanation: The default is longer than a week.

options.text: 30 days
options.explanation: By default, pages remain in Trash for 30 days.

options.text: 90 days
options.explanation: 90 days is not the default setting.

options.text: 1 year
options.explanation: A year is far beyond the default period.

questions.question: To customize the time a page stays in Trash, which sequence of actions is correct?

options.text: Settings → Appearance → Data retention → Change
options.explanation: Data retention is under Security, not Appearance.

options.text: Settings → Security → Data retention → Change → Save
options.explanation: The correct path is Settings → Security → Data retention, then Change and Save.

options.text: Workspace settings → Members → Data retention → Edit
options.explanation: Members settings do not contain data retention options.

options.text: Admin console → Trash → Set retention period
options.explanation: There is no separate Admin console for this task.

questions.question: How does the "page retention" setting differ from the "page deletion" (Trash) setting?

options.text: Retention controls how long a page stays in Trash; deletion controls how long a page is kept after being removed from Trash.
options.explanation: This reverses the definitions.

options.text: Deletion sets the period a page remains in Trash before permanent deletion; retention sets how long a permanently deleted page can still be restored by owners.
options.explanation: Deletion is about Trash duration; retention is about the post‑deletion restore window.

options.text: Both settings control the same timeframe; they are duplicate controls.
options.explanation: They serve distinct purposes.

options.text: Retention applies to shared pages only, while deletion applies to private pages only.
options.explanation: Retention applies to all pages, not just shared ones.

questions.question: If a workspace owner sets a retention period of 5 years, what happens to a page after it has been permanently deleted and the 5‑year period elapses?

options.text: The page is automatically restored to its original location.
options.explanation: Retention does not auto‑restore pages; it only allows owners to restore them within the period.

options.text: The page becomes inaccessible to everyone, including owners, and cannot be restored.
options.explanation: After the retention period ends, the page is fully purged and cannot be recovered.

options.text: The page moves from the Retained list back to the Trash for another deletion cycle.
options.explanation: Retained pages are not moved back to Trash after the period expires.

options.text: Only members who originally edited the page can still view it in a read‑only mode.
options.explanation: No access remains after the retention period ends.

questions.question: Who is able to restore a page that has been permanently deleted but is still within the retention window?

options.text: Any workspace member with edit access to the page.
options.explanation: Only owners have this elevated ability.

options.text: Only Enterprise workspace owners.
options.explanation: The article states that only Enterprise owners can view and restore permanently deleted pages during retention.

options.text: All members of the Enterprise plan, regardless of workspace ownership.
options.explanation: Regular members cannot restore retained pages.

options.text: External collaborators invited to the page.
options.explanation: External collaborators have no restoration privileges.

questions.question: When using Content Search to locate retained pages, which filter should be applied?

options.text: Page status → In Trash
options.explanation: In Trash shows only pages currently in Trash, not retained ones.

options.text: Page status → Retained pages
options.explanation: Choosing Retained pages returns pages that have been permanently deleted but are still within the retention period.

options.text: Page status → Archived
options.explanation: Archived is not a filter mentioned for this feature.

options.text: Page status → All pages
options.explanation: All pages would include active pages, not specifically retained ones.

questions.question: Before restoring a retained page, the owner can view "original permissions." What is the purpose of this step?

options.text: To change the page’s sharing settings before restoration.
options.explanation: Viewing does not change permissions; it only shows who will have access after restore.

options.text: To see which users will regain access once the page is restored to its original location.
options.explanation: The feature lets owners preview who will have access after restoration.

options.text: To permanently lock the page after restoration.
options.explanation: Locking is unrelated to viewing original permissions.

options.text: To delete the page permanently from the retention list.
options.explanation: Viewing permissions does not delete the page.

questions.question: Which of the following actions permanently removes a page from both Trash and the retention list?

options.text: Select the page in Trash and click "Restore to original location."
options.explanation: Restoring brings the page back, not delete it.

options.text: Use Content Search, click the ••• menu next to the page, and choose "Permanently delete."
options.explanation: The article describes this exact workflow for permanent deletion.

options.text: Change the retention period to 0 days.
options.explanation: The minimum allowed period is 1 day; setting to 0 is not possible.

options.text: Move the page to a Private pages folder.
options.explanation: Moving does not delete the page.

questions.question: What is the allowed range for setting a custom data retention period?

options.text: From 1 day up to 5 years
options.explanation: The maximum is 10 years, not 5.

options.text: From 1 day up to 10 years
options.explanation: The article states the custom period can be set between one day and ten years.

options.text: From 7 days up to 30 days
options.explanation: Both the lower and upper bounds are incorrect.

options.text: From 30 days up to 365 days
options.explanation: The range is broader than this and starts at one day.
